finding: Decision-makers trading between outcome probability and evidential strength select high-risk tests with low confirmation likelihood but strong evidentiary value when context emphasizes analytical rigour, but prefer low-risk tests when seeking certainty. Context and framing modulate the probability-value trade-off.

Inverse correlation between risk and benefit judgments strengthens under time pressure and reverses predictably with information that shifts overall affective impression, indicating that a shared affective evaluation drives independent-appearing judgment dimensions.

Discussed items show lower correct-memory rates than non-discussed items; conformity to partner's information during discussion predicts later memory reporting, with 85% agreement-during-discussion persisting at test, indicating genuine memory incorporation rather than pure social compliance.

Paranormal and religious believers show liberal response criterion yielding elevated detection rates for artifact faces and higher false-alarm rates on non-face pictures, with no sensitivity difference; believers rate artifact faces as more face-like and emotional than skeptics, independent of perceptual acuity.

Single identified victims elicit higher contributions and greater distress than unidentified single victims or identified groups; emotional distress mediates the effect, explaining why vivid individual representation drives larger allocation than quantity-matched group, violating economic rationality.

Incidental environmental numbers influence numeric estimates even without attention direction or recall; jersey numbers, model numbers, and restaurant names shift estimates despite being irrelevant, with effect robust against expertise and undiminished by highlighting the anchor source.
